  3. How will we integrate AI into our SAP landscape? Are we using SAP’s embedded capabilities, Joule, the new engagement layer, or external AI-powered apps running on SAP data (subject to the API policy)?
  4. How will we address change management with our business users?
  5. How will we manage AI governance, risk, and security? Using SAP’s AI simplifies the answer to this question as we cover much of this, but you still need robust AI policies in your organization

If we did not meet at Sapphire and you are interested in joining the conversation about SAP Fiori application development and how AI makes it better, faster, and cheaper, please join our monthly SAP Fiori development roundtable group or sign up for our bi-monthly newsletter.

If you are in the Silicon Valley area or can fly there on June 11, please join us for SAP Connect Day for UX. We'll go deeper into all the UX and related AI topics listed in this post.
https://events.sap.com/us-ux-innovation-day-siliconvalley-2026/en_us/home.html 

For the UX Foundation team, Peter Spielvogel.

entry.title: How SAP S/4HANA supports operational complexity in the Oil & Gas industry
entry.content:
Introduction

When I think of the most difficult fields to work with, the first one to come to my mind has to be, inevitably, Oil and Gas. I think everyone can agree with me when I say that Oil and Gas companies are extremely hard to operate, as they do so in environments where complexity is the standard: many different parts of the business depend on each other simultaneously. Daily operations depend on large networks of assets, field activities, supply chains, maintenance cycles, compliance requirements, and financial controls that all need to work together in real time.

From the outside, it is easy to assume that with these types of companies the biggest issue would be related to the size of the operations, since they tend to have thousands of employees, global operations and massive amounts of data. Nonetheless, the real challenge is making all departments, systems, and processes work together without difficulties. In other words, it is a problem of coordination, not of size.

Over the years, the system these companies manage starts changing little by little, which leads to different teams working differently instead of following one single and consistent process, and they also begin to work with varied versions of operational data. We are talking about an environment that, as time passes by, becomes harder and harder to manage.

This is where SAP S/4HANA becomes important, not because it eliminates complexity, but because it helps organizations operate through it with greater visibility and control.

When operational complexity starts creating friction

Oil and Gas companies are, in some aspects, the same as any other type of business. One of these similarities they share is that problems don’t suddenly appear one night and explode the following day. As the company evolves, small changes, which at the time may seem harmless, accumulate. Maybe a new software is added or a different way of working among employees is incorporated. Teams become more independent as they gain confidence and news acquisitions, with their respected technology and processes, are added. All these things that are necessary in the short-term cause work to become fragmented and an environment where even the most simple activities, like generating reports or tracking inventory, is hard to manage.

We have seen cases where different departments within the same company are technically using operational data, but they are not using it in a synchronized way. This is one of the main reasons the company becomes slower and less efficient. I will mention, as examples, the maintenance team, the procurement team, and the finance department. In these cases, everyone was looking at operations from different perspectives and with different update speeds. Although everyone had the information, they had it distributed across multiple systems and processes that did not communicate consistently with each other. As a result, operational planning became slower, manual intervention was required more often, and decision-making became slower as the data couldn’t be used efficiently.  Not to mention the fact that, in Oil and Gas companies, this is particularly critical because a minimal maintenance delay can affect production schedules and have financial and operational consequences simultaneously.

SAP S/4HANA helps reduce this fragmentation by acting as a shared operational environment for the whole company. Instead of every department using separate systems and isolated workflows, they all use the same operational framework. SAP integration allows areas that depend heavily on each other to exist inside one connected environment with information being consistently shared across the whole organization.

I came across a situation like this some years ago, when we worked with a global company that supports energy and field operations around the world. This company had a very large amount of operational purchasing activity, but many of the SAP processes behind it were way too manual compared to the size of the business. When the company contacted us, they had already went through problems during maintenance shutdowns because a lot of sites were sending urgent material requests at the same time through SAP.

To help with this, we automated part of the SAP purchasing and approval process so urgent requests could move faster and teams didn’t have to spend so much time manually checking and coordinating everything. This helped them process materials faster during maintenance periods and reduced a lot of repetitive admin work between operations and procurement teams.

And that is my point: SAP S/4HANA, at least in the Oil and Gas environment, is the easiest and safest way of connecting processes in real time. One of the biggest operational pressures in Oil and Gas environments comes from the fact that many activities cannot simply “wait until tomorrow.” Maintenance schedules, field operations, procurement cycles, and inventory availability are often directly connected to production continuity. Even the smallest delay can affect the whole system in these kinds of fields.

One thing is clear: operational efficiency is not only related to production itself. In fact, if production goes well it’s because there is something else functioning behind it, and that has to do with how quickly companies are able to react when conditions change. This is one of the reasons why SAP S/4HANA has become increasingly important in asset-intensive industries. It allows organizations to build more connected operational environments where information can move faster between teams and operational areas. In industries where downtime, delays, or lack of visibility can quickly translate into operational and financial consequences, having more standardized and integrated processes becomes less of a technological improvement and more of an operational necessity.

Conclusion

In Oil & Gas, circumstances almost never slow down enough to permit a full reset. Teams have to keep the wheels turning as production can’t stop, maintenance issues pop up, materials need to be in the right place, and decisions have to be made fast, all under constant stress. Minor flaws in how companies do things can snowball into much bigger problems if no one catches them early.

That’s where SAP S/4HANA really shows its value. Sure, it covers all the basics, but it goes further: it helps companies build smoother, more connected operations. Processes speed up, data gets more reliable, and teams don’t have to waste so much time tangled in paperwork or chasing down info. In a fast-paced world like Oil & Gas, being able to respond quickly and stay consistent isn’t just nice to have, it’s essential.

entry.title: Why ERP still defines the limits of AI in the enterprise
entry.content:
Introduction

There’s been a lot of noise around AI and how much it’s changing enterprise systems. It is undeniable that changes are here to stay. We’ve seen teams getting better forecasts, automating things that used to take forever, and catching issues earlier than before. Even we as an enterprise have evolved into something that I have no idea how it would function if AI suddenly disappeared one day.

But after a while, the conversation shifts. You can do countless of things with AI, and I think that is what everyone else is focusing on. And what else? What comes after checking whether AI works or not? What do you actually do once it's there? Getting recommendations is not the hard part anymore. You can have a model suggesting changes, flagging risks, or pointing at opportunities, and most of the time it makes sense. The problem shows up later, when you try to use that in real processes. That’s usually where things start to slow down, and where ERP suddenly matters again.

The Gap Between AI Insights and Execution

We’ve seen this happen more than once. Companies expect faster decisions because they have better insights, but once those decisions need to move through finance, operations, or logistics, everything gets more complicated. Systems don’t always adapt quickly, processes depend on each other, and what looked like a simple adjustment ends up taking way more effort than expected.

In many scenarios, the real challenge emerges when you have to close the execution gap, and not when you have to add more intelligence, as many people may think. It’s something that comes up all the time in conversations with our clients: the need is there, the intent is clear, but the underlying ERP landscape just isn’t ready to move at the same speed. Closing that gap between expectation and reality is where most of the real work is happening today.

The struggle that most organizations have has todo with what comes after they have produce insights. How do you execute a decision through a system that is so structured and interconnected? Is thanks to SAP S/4HANA —and similar ERP platforms— that decision has the opportunity to become reality.

On paper, the issue is simple. AI generates an insight, the business evaluates it, and then we, as organization, execute it. The thing is that, in reality, this chain encounters a vast amount of problems that lead to decisions getting delayed or partially implemented.

In one of the projects we worked on, this became very clear. The company, a global manufacturer in the energy space, was still relying on manual processes to count and track critical components. It wasn’t just slow, it also introduced errors and even safety concerns for the people involved.

By introducing AI-based image recognition, they were able to automate this process and significantly improve both productivity and safety. However, what became clear during the implementation is that the real value didn’t come from the AI model itself, but from how that information was integrated and executed across the operation. The data captured in the field needed to be synchronized with ERP systems, trigger updates in inventory, and align with warehouse and logistics processes in real time. Without that connection, the insight would have remained isolated. It was the ability to translate that intelligence into structured actions within the system that ultimately made the difference.

In another case, a client was using predictive models to anticipate demand changes and adjust procurement accordingly. The insights were accurate, but execution lagged behind. Translating those recommendations into actual purchase orders required multiple validations and manual interventions within the ERP system. By the time the adjustments were fully implemented, the conditions had already shifted. Once again, the limitation was not the quality of the insight, but the ability to execute it in time.

This is why the conversation around ERP is becoming relevant again, especially in the context of AI. It’s no longer just about having a system to run transactions, but about having a system that can absorb change and execute decisions without friction. Concepts like clean core, standardized processes, and flexible extensions are not just technical preferences anymore. They directly impact how fast and how effectively a company can act on what AI is telling them.

Conclusion

What we are seeing, then, is a shift in how these technologies relate to each other. AI is expanding what companies can see and understand about their business, giving them more options and better insights. ERP, on the other hand, continues to define what they can actually do with that information. The gap between those two layers is where many organizations are currently stuck.

The truth is that the companies that will get the most value out of AI are the ones that connect with the models to execution, and not necessarily the ones with the most advanced models. That means treating ERP not as a static backend system, but as a dynamic platform that enables change across the organization. AI might be redefining how companies think, but ERP still defines how they act. In the enterprise, action is where value is created.

entry.title: How SAPPHIRE 2026 directly influences your Cloud ERP Private modernization journey
entry.content:
Every SAP customer is on a different stage of its cloud modernization journey and those who haven’t yet started the discussions (and that could have happened due to a variety of reasons) must go through this blog.

At SAP Sapphire 2026, the conversation changed significantly.

SAP made two important announcements that together create a compelling—and urgent—case for customers running ECC On-Premise (ECC OP), S/4HANA On-Premise (S/4HANA OP), and ERP Private Cloud Edition (ERP PCE) systems to accelerate modernization toward Cloud ERP Private.

The message is becoming increasingly clear: customers who commit to modernization early will gain faster access to innovation, AI, and long-term supportability.

AI Innovation Is No Longer Reserved Only for Full Cloud Customers

One of the most impactful announcements at Sapphire 2026 was SAP’s decision to extend selected AI capabilities to ECC OP and S/4HANA OP customers.

Traditionally, many of SAP’s newest AI-driven innovations—especially those powered through Joule and cloud-native business processes—were positioned primarily for cloud ERP customers.

Now, SAP has opened a new path.

Customers running ECC or S/4HANA On-Premise systems who commit at least 50% of their on-premise maintenance base toward cloud adoption can gain access to selected AI scenarios, even while still operating their OP environments.

Based on the above, it is pertinent to share that cloud systems get all AI capabilities and on-premises systems will get a sub-set. It requires customers and SAP effort to make agents work on-premises, driving our cloud and on-premises differentiation. Note this applies to agents running on ERP systems, not migration agents.

To help accelerate the impact of AI to our customers’ businesses, SAP experts will help enable agentic AI for our new and existing Cloud ERP Private customers by activating up to 3 Joule Assistants in their ERP at no additional cost. Customers can select Joule Assistants that are available in their environments which will support common workflows such as finance, HR and supply chain.

Lastly, it is important to state that there is no change in SAP’s AI policy as AI agents require standardized systems to operate reliably, and the cloud is where SAP can guarantee this foundation, so the cloud remains our strategic focus. The activation of agents on on-premises systems requires services and engineering resources which we are offering to customers that commit to convert at least 50% of their current maintenance fee to the cloud. For those customers, we’ll provide strategic AI use cases so they can begin using and getting value from AI faster. Capabilities may vary based on technical requirements, level of customizations on existing systems, and may require underlying application as required by the agent. Please reach out to your SAP contact for more information.

Essentially, the SAP announcement acknowledges an important market reality:

  • Customers still need to derive innovation value during transition years.
  • AI adoption cannot wait for the “final phase” of ERP transformation.

However, there is an important nuance.

These AI capabilities will still come with boundaries and limitations compared to fully cloud-native environments. The deepest innovation velocity will continue to happen in Cloud ERP Private and public cloud solutions. Which means this announcement should not be interpreted as a reason to delay modernization indefinitely.

Instead, it should be seen as a bridge. A bridge that allows customers to start realizing AI value while they accelerate toward their longer-term cloud ERP architecture.

The ERP PCE Transition Option May Not Apply to Everyone

Another major development shaping customer strategy is the upcoming SAP ERP, private edition, transition option service expected around 2028.

This service is intended to help ERP PCE customers transition more smoothly toward Cloud ERP Private.

At first glance, this sounds reassuring for customers who believe they have more time but there is a critical detail that organizations should not ignore:

The transition option service is expected to include a 2 TB database size criterion.

That means not every ERP PCE customer may qualify and that changes the planning equation entirely.

Organizations that assume they can “wait until 2028” may discover later that:

  • Their system size doesn’t reach the system eligibility criteria
  • Their landscape complexity requires longer preparation cycles
  • Data reduction and archiving activities take years, not months
  • Skilled transformation resources become increasingly constrained as deadlines approach

In other words, the later customers begin, the fewer options they may ultimately have.

ECC and S/4HANA OP Customers Should Treat This as a Strategic Window

The Sapphire 2026 announcements create an interesting transition period.

SAP is effectively giving customers:

  1. Early AI access during the transition journey
  2. A modernization path toward Cloud ERP Private
  3. Incentives to begin cloud commitment now rather than later

Modernization Is No Longer Just About End of Maintenance

For many years, ERP modernization discussions revolved around maintenance deadlines.

That framing is now outdated.

The real differentiator moving forward is innovation velocity.

SAP’s Sapphire 2026 announcements signal a future where AI, automation, and cloud-delivered capabilities become central to enterprise competitiveness—even for customers still operating hybrid landscapes.

But hybrid access is unlikely to be the destination.

It is the transition phase.

And for ECC OP, S/4HANA OP, and ERP PCE customers alike, the smartest move may be to use this phase not as a reason to delay transformation, but as an opportunity to accelerate it thoughtfully and strategically.

entry.title: ATUL Architecture #2 — Event-Driven Utilities: Designing Real-Time Flows on SAP BTP
entry.content:
ATUL Architecture #2 — Event-Driven Utilities: Designing Real-Time Flows on SAP BTP

Introduction

Most of the utilities organization did not build batch-driven systems because they were wrong. They built them because, for many years, batch processing was enough and serving the business need to generate the mass customer bills and satisfying other business processes like payment processing, return and refunds etc.

Meter reads come daily. Billing jobs ran overnight. Outage analysis could wait a few minutes. But now with the new AI Era this world is changing fast.

That’s means today, utilities operate in an environment where:

  • Smart meters generate continuous streams of events.
  • EV charging creates unpredictable load spikes,
  • Distributed energy resources change grid behavior dynamically,
  • Customers expect instant updates and near real-time visibility,

In this environment, delays are no longer just technical limitations — they directly impact operations and customer experience.

Let’s understand this with a simple example occurs daily.

A customer loses power at 2:05 PM, the smart meter detects the outage immediately.

But the utility system processes that information at 2:30 PM, the question is why and the answer is because the event is sitting inside a scheduled batch cycle. Now that 25-minute delay creates:

  • Slower outage response.
  • Increased call center traffic.
  • Poor customer experience.
  • Potential regulatory exposure.

This is where event-driven architecture becomes critical. And this is where SAP BTP can play a major role in modern utility architecture.

This is exactly where event-driven architecture comes in—and where SAP BTP starts to make a real difference.

1.       Why Event-Driven Utilities Matter (In Real Life)

Utilities are no longer operating predictable, centralized grids.

The grid is becoming:

  • Distributed
  • Dynamic
  • High-volume
  • Event-heavy

Millions of devices are continuously generating signals:

  • Smart meters
  • Grid sensors
  • DER systems
  • EV charging stations
  • Field devices

Traditional batch systems struggle in this environment — not because the technology is bad, but because the operating model has changed.

Event-driven architecture changes the model completely.

That’s means Instead of waiting for scheduled processing windows or batch, systems react when events occur. In simple terms real time processing windows.

That enables:

  • Faster outage detection
  • Near real-time billing updates
  • Automated operational workflows
  • Immediate exception handling
  • Better grid visibility

This shift is not only about speed. It is about building utilities that can respond continuously instead of periodically.

2.       Where SAP BTP Fits In (Simple View)

Think of SAP BTP Event Mesh as the traffic controller for events. In many utilities, integration landscapes become tightly coupled over time.

One system talks directly to another.  Then another connection gets added. Then another.

Eventually, even small changes become difficult and risky.

This is where SAP Event Mesh on SAP BTP becomes important.

Think of Event Mesh as the event backbone of the enterprise.

Instead of systems communicating directly:

  • Systems publish events
  • Interested systems subscribe to them
  • Processing becomes decoupled and scalable

In a utility environment:

  • AMI systems publish meter events
  • Grid applications publish operational events
  • SAP S/4HANA consumes only business-relevant events
  • Other BTP services enrich, filter, or analyze the data

This creates flexibility that traditional point-to-point integration cannot provide.

But there is an important architectural reality here:

Poor event design creates the same problems as poor API design.

If event topics, queue structures, and filtering rules are not designed carefully, Event Mesh can quickly become another bottleneck.

3.       A Simple End-to-End Example (AMI → BTP → S/4HANA)

Let’s walk through a simple but real-time business case or scenario.

Scenario of Power outage detected

  • Step 1 — Meter detects outage: A smart meter immediately detects loss of power.
  • Smart meter sends an outage event immediately.
  • This goes to SAP BTP (via MQTT or API).
  • At this point, no batch job is waiting to process the information later. The event is available immediately.

 

  • Step 2 — SAP BTP Processes the Event

From there:

  • Integration services validate the payload.
  • Business rules evaluate the event.
  • Additional context is added.

For example:

  • Is this a single-home outage?
  • Or are multiple meters reporting outages from the same feeder?

This is where intelligence starts to matter. BTP services can:

  • Correlate events,
  • Filter noise
  • Detect patterns
  • Trigger automation

In many architectures, this becomes the real operational brain of the utility platform.

  • Step 3 — SAP S/4HANA Executes the Business Process

 Once the event is validated:

  • A work order can be created automatically
  • Field crews can be dispatched
  • Customers can receive notifications
  • Device management processes can begin
  • Billing or service workflows can update

S/4HANA remains the system of record. But SAP BTP handles the real-time orchestration layer around it. That separation is important for scalability.

4.        What Actually Changes (Batch vs Real-Time)

Traditional Batch Model

  • Meter data processed hourly or daily
  • Exceptions reviewed manually
  • Outage visibility delayed
  • Billing corrections handled later
  • Integrations tightly coupled

Event-Driven Model

  • Continuous event processing
  • Automated workflow triggering
  • Near real-time outage detection
  • Dynamic operational response
  • Scalable publish/subscribe integrations

But there is an important operational truth many projects have discovered late:

Real-time systems expose process problems that batch systems quietly hide.

When systems react immediately:

  • Data quality issues surface faster, and this is real point of having BTP.
  • Business rule conflicts become visible, immediate effect/result.
  • Operational inefficiencies can no longer stay hidden behind overnight processing.

This is why event-driven transformation is both technical and organizational.

5.       Where Things Usually Go Wrong (From Experience)

Mostly in real projects, challenges show up quickly:

  • Event storms: Millions of AMI events can flood the platform during outages or restoration cycles. Without proper throttling and filtering, downstream systems become overwhelmed.
  • Poor event design: If event topics are designed inconsistently:
    • Consumers become tightly coupled
    • Governance becomes difficult
    • Reusability disappears
  • Overloading S/4HANA: Not every event belongs inside ERP. One of the biggest mistakes is forwarding raw operational noise directly into S/4HANA. ERP should receive business-relevant outcomes — not every technical signal.
  • Duplicate processing: In distributed event systems, duplicate events are normal. Architectures must be designed with idempotency in mind from the beginning.

And the biggest one:

Business rules have become more complex than expected. In most implementations, BTP becomes the brain, not just the pipe.

6.        Architecture Principle That Matters Most

The biggest mindset about this modern architecture shift is this:

SAP BTP should not behave like traditional middleware.

And it should be important to understand since most of the client leadership want new system but same like old. It should behave like:

  • An orchestration platform
  • An event intelligence layer
  • A real-time decision platform

That is a very different architectural role.

7.       Key Patterns That Actually Work

From a practitioner point of view, these patterns matter:

  • Use publish/subscribe, not point-to-point integrations
  • Filter and enrich events before they reach S/4HANA
  • Keep S/4HANA focused on business transactions, not raw events
  • Design for idempotency (avoid duplicates)
  • Design events for reuse and scalability
  • Treat Event Mesh as a platform capability, not just middleware

8.       Final Thought

Important things we need to understand as architects and as organization leaders, moving to event-driven architecture is not simply a technology modernization effort. It will change how utilities operate, most of the way business processes execute may change or enhance. That’s result an organization becoming:

  • Faster decisions which will improve business process.
  • Better customer experience.
  • More automation, less manual work.
  • Less dependency on batch cycles.

But it also requires discipline in design. Because one system reacts instantly:


Its means There is very little room for poorly designed processes, noisy integrations, or unclear ownership.

In real-time utilities, architecture quality becomes operational quality.

Next in the Series

ATUL Architecture #3

In the next article, we move closer to the operational grid layer:

  • OT/IT convergence
  • MQTT ingestion patterns
  • IEC 61850 integration
  • Substation-to-cloud flows
  • Security and isolation models.

This is where utility architecture starts moving from enterprise latency… to grid latency.

entry.title: Enabling Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS) by SAP solution capabilities
entry.content:
This article intends to give an overview what SAP solutions enable the value chain for Carbon Capture & Storage. It’s meant to be an outside-in view and food for thought.

Enabling Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS) by SAP solution capabilities for Energy companies: An Overview 

Global warming is one of the most urgent challenges of our time. Over the past century, average global temperatures have risen significantly, driven primarily by the accumulation of greenhouse gases (GHGs) in the atmosphere. Among this, carbon dioxide (CO₂) is the most influential due to its volume of emissions and its long atmospheric lifetime.

Despite measures to reduce energy consumption, CO2 emissions from industrial operations, transport, and households keep rising. While expectations for reduction are high – supported by electrification and renewable energies like wind, solar, and green H2 – certain "hard-to-abate" industries such as cement, iron, and steel face significant hurdles in reducing emissions effectively and efficiently. For these industries, where emissions are unavoidable, capturing CO₂ directly at the source and securely storing it underground helps stop it from entering the atmosphere and plays a vital role in emissions reduction.

Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS) has evolved from experimental pilot projects into operational infrastructure. Recent years have witnessed breakthrough developments across the entire CCS value chain, from next-generation capture technologies achieving 95%+ efficiency to cross-border transport networks and proven geological storage at commercial scale.

Key global storage projects now demonstrate the viability of CCS:

  • Norway: The Northern Lights project has established Europe's first open-source CO₂ storage infrastructure, accepting emissions from multiple industrial emitters.
  • United States: The Petra Nova and Illinois Industrial CCS projects have validated large-scale capture from power and ethanol production.
  • Australia: The Gorgon project stores millions of tons annually in offshore formations.
  • United Kingdom: The Acorn and HyNet clusters are mobilizing industrial decarbonization at regional scale.

What is Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS)?

Carbon Capture & Storage is a comprehensive value chain that involves capturing CO2 emissions from industrial or energy-related sources, transporting it to a storage site, and permanently sequestering it in deep underground geological formations. These reservoirs often located in offshore subseafloor formations, ensure the carbon cannot contribute to atmospheric warming.

Typically, a CCS value chain consists of three major processes.

  1. Capture
    CO₂ is separated from exhaust gases using capture technologies. Modern efficiency often exceeds 90%.
  2. Transport
    Captured CO₂ is compressed and transported to the reservoir – typically via pipelines, but also by ship, truck, or rail. This often involves two transportation legs: First, from the capture location to a terminal (the handover from CO2 emitter to storage company). And second, from the terminal into the subsea reservoir.
  3. Storage
    CO₂ is injected into deep geological formations such as depleted oil and gas fields or saline aquifers. These formations are well characterized, have secure caprock layers, and have naturally held fluids and gases for millions of years.

Figure 1 shows how the value chain is modeled at Equinor in their Northern Lights CCS project.

Figure 1 - CCS value chain - Capture, Transport, Store (source: Equinor)

Current CCS projects share common attributes: integrated infrastructure connecting multiple emitters to shared storage, cross-party collaboration spanning industry and government, and sophisticated monitoring and verification systems. However, as CCS transitions from demonstration to deployment, a critical challenge emerges operational and digital integration.

 

Transport and Store as a service – A Business Model for oil & gas companies

In a full chain business model, a single company controls every stage of the CCS process across Capture, Transport and Storage. However, a decoupled model is emerging as the dominant approach for oil & gas Industry.

Oil & gas companies are uniquely positioned to deliver CCS storage solutions. They possess decades of experiences in subsurface engineering, largescale project execution, huge offshore infrastructure operations, seismic studies, reservoir modelling and bulk transportation of oil & gas. In addition, they have access to depleted reservoirs and understand their specific geological situations. Therefore, their focus is on transportation & storing of CO2, rather than on capturing it.  

We call the corresponding business model Transport and Store as a service:

Figure 2 - Transport and Store as a Service

CAPTURE

  • The oil & gas company is not involved in capturing CO2.
  • Emitting companies or specialized firms capture the CO2

TRANSPORT

  • Transport CO2 to a predefined transit point, typically a harbor or pipeline terminal.
  • At the transit point, handover and ownership change take place, and the oil & gas business model starts. The oil & gas company does bulk transportation from transit point to the subsurface reservoir.

STORE

  • Inject and store delivered CO2 quantity in subsurface reservoir.
  • Ensure E2E sustainability and safety compliance of reservoir and stored CO2.
  • Long-term monitoring and closure

In this service model, industrial customers pay a service fee per ton of CO2 delivered to the transit point. They will get a validated CO2 certificate for the delivered quantity.  

This model is now entering early commercial stages, as demonstrated by projects such as Northern Lights in Norway, HyNet in the UK, and Porthos in the Netherlands.
This paper focuses on this model, Transport & Storage as a Service, mapping the critical business capabilities and the enabling SAP S/4HANA solutions required to establish a unified digital core that connects operational reality to regulatory compliance and financial accounting.

Required Business Capabilities for Offering CCS Storage Services

To operate CCS at scale in a Transport & Store as a service business model, an oil and gas company needs to have strong capabilities across technical, commercial, operational, regulatory, and digital dimensions. Key business capabilities during revenue realization phase include:

 

Commercial & Customer Management

  • Customer onboarding and service delivery
  • Manage customer contracts across their lifecycle
  • Pricing and billing of CO₂ storage services

Transportation, Storage & Safety

  • Manage various types of transports
  • Quality & Inventory Management
  • Dangerous goods handling
  • CO₂ Injection
  • Monitoring, reporting and verification

Asset Lifecycle Management

  • Maintenance, reliability, and integrity
  • Large-scale project management
  • Procurement and logistics

Regulatory, Environmental & Compliance Management

  • Permitting and environmental impact assessments
  • Compliance with CO₂ storage regulations
  • Sustainability reporting and audits

Finance, Billing & JV Management

  • Capital project accounting
  • Multi‑party JV structures common in CCS hubs

 

Mapping CCS Business Capabilities to SAP S/4HANA

SAP S/4HANA and other SAP cloud solutions provide the digital foundation for CCS operators to follow the molecules along the value chain and to integrate financials, operations, logistics, compliance, and sustainability throughout the CCS service value chain.

Figure 3 SAP Solution Mapping

It all starts with aligning and setting up contracts with emitters in SAP S/4HANA Sales, with volume commitments, quality specifications and pricing formulas for delivering CO2. With that in place, emitters/customers can start nominating CO2 quantities for storing. SAP supports the complete contract-to-cash process, including logistics coordination and settlement and billing of CO2 quantities delivered, and the entire storage service.

SAP Hydrocarbon Supply and Primary Distribution solution manages transportation of CO2 by various means including vessels and pipelines. Transportation sourcing and inventory planning, schedule pipeline transport, execute the actual transport, manage tickets, claims and shipping costs.

SAP Quality Management enables organizations to plan quality requirements by specifications, and control characteristics for CO2 quality delivered, suppliers, and processes. The solution supports quality inspections throughout the supply chain, including goods receipt of CO2 and manages nonconformances through structured quality notifications that drive preventive actions. CO2 storage operators face a need for clear CO₂ quality specifications and contractual alignment with emitters and registering of injected volumes and composition.
For MRO, it is tightly integrated with procurement, inventory, and logistics, ensuring that quality decisions directly impact operational processes.

CO2 is a hazardous material. The SAP Environment, Health and Safety (SAP EHS) solution helps organizations manage environmental compliance, occupational health, and workplace safety within a single integrated system. The solution supports incident management, risk assessment, and preventive measures to reduce workplace hazards and improve employee safety all along the CO2 value chain. SAP EHS enables compliance with regulatory requirements by managing permits, emissions, hazardous substances, and safety data documentation. It provides structured processes for audits, inspections, and corrective actions, ensuring transparency and traceability. Integrated analytics and reporting deliver insights into EHS performance, supporting risk reduction, compliance assurance, and continuous improvement.

SAP Green Token enables companies to digitally track and allocate sustainability attributes, alongside physical material flows. It provides blockchain-based end‑to‑end transparency and auditable evidence of CO2 quantities and qualities in multi‑tier supply chains across capture, transport and storage processes. SAP Green Token supports credible sustainability claims by ensuring traceability, auditability, and compliance with certification and regulatory frameworks. By integrating with existing ERP and supply chain systems, it helps organizations operationalize communicate verified environmental impacts to customers and stakeholders.
SAP Advanced Analytics and SAP Sustainability Control Tower, SAP’s sustainability suite works together to enable measurement, governance, and value-chain transparency that underpin Carbon Capture and Storage programs. It centralizes ESG targets and performance, letting you track CCS as an abatement initiative, model scenarios, and report progress with audit-ready monitoring, reporting and verification (MRV). 
CCS storage operators must comply with geological storage permitting frameworks (such as the EU CCS Directive or EPA's Underground Injection Control Class VI rules in the US), which govern site selection, injection operations, and long-term CO₂ containment monitoring. High transparency, audit readiness, data management, and long-term record-keeping obligations are key, from quantities received to injected and eventually leaked to the surface.

In the process of CO2 transportation and storage, big technical installations and equipment are involved which require full attention to operate them efficiently and safely. SAP Enterprise Asset Management supervises asset health status, manages maintenance processes and orchestrates corresponding procurement and logistics for MRO materials.

  • SAP Enterprise Asset Management (EAM): Manages the entire lifecycle of CCS infrastructure, from pipelines to injection wells. It coordinates preventive and corrective maintenance to maximize the uptime and safety of these critical assets.
  • SAP Asset Performance Management (APM) uses sensor data (IoT) with maintenance history to assess asset health. By using predictive analytics, operators can prioritize interventions on offshore equipment, reducing unplanned downtime and environmental risks.
  • SAP Field Logistics: Tracks spare parts and materials across remote locations and offshore platforms, ensuring that technicians have the right tools available to minimize operational delays.

To scale Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) successfully, organizations must orchestrate massive volumes of operational data, maintain high-risk infrastructure, and ensure total compliance across the value chain. SAP Business AI meets this challenge by embedding intelligence directly into the SAP ecosystem, transforming raw data into automated, actionable insights.

Built on the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), this AI framework supports three critical pillars of CCS operations:

  • Supply Chain & Logistics Visibility: AI analyzes real-time logistics and market data to provide end-to-end visibility. For CCS, this means optimizing the midstream transport of compressed CO2 via ship, rail, or pipeline.
  • Intelligent Asset Management: By monitoring sensor data from capture plants and injection wells, AI identifies failure risks before they occur. This predictive maintenance is vital for CCS infrastructure, where downtime can lead to significant environmental and financial penalties.
  • The Rise of Joule Agents: SAP’s generative AI assistant, Joule, acts as an agent orchestrator. It allows operators to query complex CCS data using natural language, significantly reducing the manual effort required to manage global sequestration projects.
  • ESG What if scenarios: As CCS companies are a highly regulated industry means that they also need to report on their own emissions. SAP Sustainability Control Tower together with SAP Analytics Cloud serves as the central hub for ESG data. The addition of AI makes it possible to go beyond reporting but also looks into what if scenarios. What happens if we increase the injection by 15%, what would the effect on the emissions?

Conclusion

Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS) is a crucial technology for decarbonization, particularly for industries with limited alternatives for emission reduction. Oil and gas companies are uniquely positioned to lead this transition due to their subsurface expertise and extensive offshore infrastructure. However, a successful CCS venture is more than a technical feat; it is a sophisticated service business that demands robust strategic, commercial, and digital capabilities. SAP S/4HANA provides the integrated solution landscape necessary to connect operational reality with logistics flows, regulatory compliance and financial integrity, creating a scalable foundation for the future of carbon management.

entry.title: Announcement: Register to the new CEI project for SAP Application Interface Framework
entry.content:
SAP Application Interface Framework (AIF) is an integral part of SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud for implementing, monitoring, and managing application interfaces.

In production environments, when a large number of interface messages fail, manual error diagnosis and error resolution can lead to significant effort and time. To address this, we plan to enhance SAP Application Interface Framework to support an AI-assisted error remediation framework that transforms error handling from tedious manual work into intelligent, automated recovery.

To gather feedback on those planned product improvements, we will run the Customer Engagement Initiative (CEI) project AI-Assisted Error Remediation for SAP Application Interface Framework. The CEI project aims to collect feedback on the concept and a first proof of concept at an early stage, focusing on:

  • Error classification: How AI-driven error classification can reduce manual effort.
  • Remediation automation: Validating the remediation approach against real customer scenarios.
  • Autonomous vs. guided remediation: Understanding customer preferences for automatic execution versus human-in-the-loop approval.

If you like to join the upcoming SAP Application Interface Framework influence project, please register here (link leads to the customer influence page).

Registration is open from May 18th until June 19th 2026.

Once, the registration closes, we will invite you for an initial call where we go through the scope and the prerequisites of the feedback project. This will be around end of June, begin of July, the actual feedback sessions will start in July.

We are looking forward to your active participation and valuable feedback.

entry.title: Como integrar um serviço BTP Cloud Foundry a um ambiente S4HANA On-premise
entry.content:
O cenário é o seguinte: você quer hospedar um app Fiori Freestyle no BTP Cloud Foundry, que vai se conectar a um sistema S4HANA on-premise e será utilizado pelos usuários finais.

Pode parecer bem específico, mas isso envolve um entendimento de várias camadas de arquitetura, então vale a pena tomar um tempo para entender isso.

Esse objetivo envolve criar serviços auxiliares, que vão se comunicar com o S4HANA por intermédio do Cloud Connector.

O primeiro passo é instalar o SAP Cloud Connector, pode ser no mesmo servidor que hospeda o S4HANA ou em outro servidor na mesma rede.

Ele é um programa Java, então é necessário instalar a JVM. Tecnicamente pode ser usada qualquer JVM, porém a SAP também possui suas próprias versões da JVM, então é recomendável usar elas. Os detalhes estão nesse link: https://help.sap.com/docs/connectivity/sap-btp-connectivity-cf/installation.

Com ele rodando no ambiente, você vai configurar os dados da sua BTP subaccount (ele pode se conectar em mais de uma subaccount).

A conexão entre os serviços da subaccount e o S4HANA vão ser intermediadas por essa conexão com o Cloud Connector.

Quando falamos em integração de serviços externos com um ambiente SAP, não podemos ignorar a parte de autenticação e segurança. Existem várias formas de configuração, mas a mais “nativa” para esse cenário seria a Principal Propagation.

Para entender a arquitetura, mentalize o seguinte fluxo, supondo que no Cloud Foundry você escolha o Node como backend:

  • O usuário abre a aplicação Fiori, que tenta puxar uma informação de uma API do S4HANA através do path relativo (Ex: /sap/opu/odata4/sap/z*);

  • Essa requisição cai no container Node, e se o usuário não estiver logado, ele é redirecionado para uma url de autenticação, onde é informado seu email e senha do Identity Provider (que pode ser da SAP, Azure, AWS, etc.);

  • Essa autenticação gera um token com informações do usuário, que é enviado ao Node;

  • O Node, tendo o token e o caminho da API, direciona ambos para um destination, que aponta para a conexão criada no Cloud Connector;

  • O Cloud Connector pega uma informação desse token, por exemplo o e-mail do usuário, e gera um certificado digital temporário;

  • Esse certificado é enviado em uma conexão https para o endpoint real do S4HANA

  • O S4HANA valida o certificado e mapeia ele para um usuário interno

  • A partir daí a lógica da API odata segue normal, baseada no que o usuário pode fazer, e a resposta é devolvida;

Existe um outro blog post que também explica sobre o Principal Propagation. De qualquer forma vou explicar aqui, mas se quiser conferir, o link é: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/principal-propagation-setup-between-s4hana-and-btp-portal/ba-p/13522753.

Para tornar isso possível, após conectar o Cloud Connector na subaccount, o próximo passo vai ser configurar os certificados digitais dele, na área “Connector > Configuration > On-premises”.

Nesse painel existem 2 tipos de certificado digital: um é para o proxy do Cloud Connector, esse é denominado “System certificate”, e o outro é o que o Cloud Connector utiliza para gerar os certificados temporários, denominado “CA Certificate”.

Nessa mesma página é possível configurar quais informações extrair do token para gerar o certificado, na área do Principal Propagation.

Após isso, é hora de configurar a conexão com o S4HANA on-premise.

Primeiramente, o Cloud Connector precisa confiar no certificado https do servidor S4HANA. Por padrão ele só confia em uma lista definida de certificados, denominada “Back-end Trust Store”. Você pode fazer lá o upload manualmente, ou pode também desativar essa opção, assim ele vai confiar em qualquer certificado do back-end.

Feito isso, na área da sua subaccount, página “Cloud to On-Premises”, vai ser configurado os detalhes específicos do proxy.

A conexão deve ser necessariamente via HTTPS, pois é assim que o Cloud Connector consegue trocar o certificado gerado com o S4HANA de forma nativa através do próprio protocolo.

Exemplo (na edição ele exibe como HTTP, mas foi criada como HTTPS):

Feito isso, a próxima etapa é fazer o Cloud Connector confiar no token gerado pelo serviço de autenticação da sua subaccount.

Para isso, nessa mesma página do “Cloud to On-Premises” existe também a aba do “Principal propagation”, onde você deve sincronizar. A primeira execução é manual, depois dela você pode marcar a opção para sincronizar automaticamente.

 

Em resumo, até aqui já temos uma instância do Cloud Connector rodando na rede, vinculada a uma subaccount BTP, com os certificados system e CA criados, e configurada para se conectar ao S4HANA on-premise via https.

Agora faltam 2 coisas: configurar o S4HANA para confiar no Cloud Connector, e configurar os serviços no BTP Cloud Foundry para que seja possível essa integração.

Para que o S4HANA confie no Cloud Connector, é necessário ajustar alguns parâmetros de perfil do sistema (isso envolve configurações BASIS).

O primeiro ponto é fazer ele confiar no certificado digital de sistema do Cloud Connector, existem 2 formas de fazer isso:

  • Com os antigos parâmetros “icm/HTTPS/trust_client_with_subject” e “icm/HTTPS/trust_client_with_issuer”, que são configurados via transação RZ10. Esses parâmetros definem o padrão do emissor e assunto do certificado digital que o sistema pode confiar, e é um valor único. Eles são do tipo estático, então é necessário reiniciar a instância do S4 para aplicar as edições.

Exemplo:

  • Com o novo parâmetro “icm/trusted_reverse_proxy_<x>”, onde o “<x>” é um número sequencial começando do 0. Ele tem esse padrão porque dessa forma é possível configurar mais de um certificado confiado pelo servidor, diferente dos anteriores que só permitiam um valor. Ele é do tipo dinâmico, então não é sempre necessário reiniciar a instância para aplicar, e pode ser alterado a partir da transação RZ11 (de forma indireta). Mais informações sobre ele no link: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_NETWEAVER_AS_ABAP_752/077f95b579a0474fbef5d520be10b680/3bea0adde9a54db5843be8f7bf01fefa.html.

Exemplo:

Também é necessário importar os dois certificados do Cloud Connector na transação STRUST, no servidor SSL Padrão.

Feito isso, o sistema S4HANA confia no certificado do Cloud Connector, mas ainda não consegue mapear os certificados temporários para usuários dentro do SAP. Aí entra a configuração da transação CERTRULE, onde você faz o upload de um certificado temporário gerado pelo Cloud Connector e pode mapear ele para usuários através de regras, ou diretamente.

Exemplo:

Para habilitar isso também é necessário configurar um parâmetro de perfil, o “login/certificate_mapping_rulebased”:

No lado do S4HANA é basicamente isso, agora só falta configurar os serviços dentro do BTP.

O cenário pensado nesse tutorial é de uma aplicação CAP Fiori rodando no Cloud Foudry, então vamos criar os serviços no mesmo space da aplicação.

São basicamente 3 serviços:

  • destination: Conceito similar ao da transação SM59, guarda dentro dele o destino para a conexão do Cloud Connector. Ele possui painel próprio para configurações.

  • connectivity: É um serviço sem configuração nenhuma, que apenas serve como ponte entre a aplicação e o serviço de destination.

  • xsuaa: É o que faz a ponte da aplicação com o provedor de identidade. Ele é configurado apenas via json, onde é possível definir o app que vai consumi-lo, as urls que pode redirecionar após autenticar o usuário, e até mesmo criar novas roles dentro do BTP, que podem posteriormente ser incluídas em Role collections e atribuídas a usuários.

Feito isso, só falta configurar a conexão dentro do painel do “Destination Service”. Lá você vai criar um destination com o Proxy type “OnPremise” e mapear para a url virtual gerada no painel do Cloud Connector, com autenticação via “PrincipalPropagation”.

Exemplo:

O app node deve estar configurado para que o destino do S4HANA seja esse Name que você configurou acima (não confunda isso com a url virtual ou com o serviço destination).

Agora para integrar tudo isso, basta a aplicação estar configurada para fazer o binding nesses 3 serviços. Isso é feito através do arquivo “manifest.yml”, usado na hora de subir o app no Cloud Foundry.

Exemplo:

Finalmente, seu app Node estará pronto para se autenticar e consumir serviços odata do seu S4HANA On-Premise.

 

Em resumo, ao seguir esse tutorial você foi capaz de entender e configurar as camadas de integração entre um app no BTP Cloud Foundry e um sistema S4Hana On-Premise (via api OData).

Talvez essas ferramentas, configurações e caminhos específicos mudem com o tempo, o mais importante é entender a arquitetura e os conceitos envolvidos nessa integração.

Obrigado pela leitura, espero ter ajudado 😄

entry.title: AI Needs Clean Data. Business Networks Stop It Getting Dirty in the First Place
entry.content:
There is a concept in professional kitchens called mise en place, everything in its place before service begins. The best chefs do not start cooking and hope the ingredients sort themselves out mid-order. The prep happens first, deliberately and completely, so that when the pressure is on the kitchen can move at pace without chaos.

For some reason my brain decided to align that principle to the concept of data quality and its use in agentic AI. I finally figured out what caused the spark, Zero100's AI-Ready Data Imperative report which defines the material difference between data that informs a decision, data that triggers a recommendation, and data that an agent acts on autonomously (there is also a nice podcast on the topic). A dashboard built on imperfect data gets questioned by a human who applies judgement and context. An agent built on imperfect data acts, reroutes the shipment, adjusts the replenishment signal, commits to the supplier, before anyone has noticed the input was wrong. The same ambiguity that a person absorbs in a meeting becomes operational risk the moment a system is doing the acting.

The same research found that only 15% of organisations consider their data foundations execution-ready. Let that land for a moment, because that means 85% of the AI programmes being announced, funded, and celebrated right now are being built on ground that cannot support the weight being placed on them.

The honest reality is that the foundations have not been prepared for this level of trust, and most organisations are not yet having that conversation clearly enough to know it.

The instinctive response is to treat this as a reason to delay. Clean everything first, then deploy agents. That instinct is understandable, but most organisations do not have the luxury of acting on it. Board pressure is intense, Wall Street is rewarding AI narratives and punishing silence, and vendors are racing to rebadge everything as agentic. The result is a top-down mandate to move fast colliding with a bottom-up reality that the foundations are not ready. Waiting for the data estate to be fully clean is not a strategy, it is an indefinite deferral dressed up as diligence. I have yet to meet the enterprise that finished a data quality programme and declared victory.

So the real question is not how do we clean everything but what do we actually need to clean to make a specific decision trustworthy.

Zero100's research consistently finds that the list of data inputs that actually determine the outcome of any given decision is shorter than most people expect. Work backwards from the decision, inventory rebalancing, late shipment triage, supplier payment timing, and ask which fields, if wrong or late, make the agent's action wrong or late. Typically ten to twenty fields. They call these Golden Inputs, not because they are glamorous, but because they carry disproportionate weight and deserve disproportionate attention. Govern those fields with the same rigour applied to operational KPIs, and you have a foundation an agent can genuinely act on. Leave them in the same state as everything else, and you have an agent making fast, confident, wrong decisions.

Here is where the argument takes a turn that most conversations about data readiness miss entirely. In supply chain, the triggering event is almost always external. A supplier confirmation, a carrier delay, an invoice exception, a forecast revision. The decisions agents will be asked to make are not primarily driven by data that originates inside your four walls. They are driven by signals arriving from outside them. Which means the most consequential Golden Inputs in any supply chain decision are very often external data fields, not internal ones.

This matters because it reframes the entire data cleansing challenge. Internal data cleansing is valuable but it is a continuous battle, because dirty data keeps entering the estate faster than it can be cleaned. Every new transaction, every supplier update, every manual entry is a potential source of contamination. It is the culinary equivalent of trying to keep a kitchen clean while unchecked deliveries arrive throughout service.

Business networks change that dynamic fundamentally. Data arriving over a governed network is structured, validated, and clean at the point of entry. Those fields do not join the cleansing backlog because they never needed cleansing in the first place. Think of it as the difference between a kitchen that inspects every ingredient as it arrives from the supplier, and one that waits until service to discover the fish was not fresh. Quality is established before it enters the kitchen, not discovered as a problem halfway through cooking.

This is where SAP Business Network plays a defining role. With millions of trading partners already connected and transacting through structured, governed document flows, the network is delivering AI-ready Golden Inputs at scale today, not as a future state. Purchase orders, advance ship notices, invoices, confirmations, each carrying their critical fields, all arriving clean by design. For organisations building toward agentic AI in supply chain, that is not a nice-to-have, it is the shortest path between ambition and trustworthy autonomous action.

The path to agentic AI is not a transformation programme. It is a sequence of small, governed steps, each one earning the right to the next. Start with a single decision, identify its Golden Inputs, and rather than beginning an internal cleansing project of uncertain duration, ask which of those inputs arrives via a business network and is therefore already clean, already structured, already trustworthy. In supply chain that answer is often most of them.

The problem in most kitchens is not the quality of the chefs. It is that the ingredients keep arriving without quality checks, and the team spends half its time sorting through the delivery before it can start cooking. The best kitchens do not just prep well, they have rigorous supplier standards, so the ingredients arrive ready to use.

That is what business networks do for agentic AI. Not a better mop. A cleaner kitchen.

entry.title: Service Bindings with OAuth 2.0 for SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, Cloud Edition
entry.content:
This post outlines the procedure to specify client credentials required for SAP S/4HANA or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition systems to communicate with SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition, using OAuth 2.0 client authentication. OAuth 2.0 provides a secure, token-based approach that is required for newer integration scenarios with SAP BTP services.

Prerequisites

Before starting the procedure, ensure the following requirements are met:

  • Initial setup in SAP BTP has been performed manually or via the Set Up an Account for SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, Cloud Edition booster
  • Required country/region-specific processes are active for your subaccount.
  • SAP Note 3540443 – Electronic Communication Integration Framework: Implementation Overview has been implemented.
  • You have the Space Developer or Org Manager role in your SAP BTP subaccount.

Note: If initial setup for a process or business system is already complete, it does not need to be repeated for additional processes in the same subaccount region.

Step 1: Create the Service Binding

    • Navigate to the Subaccount — In the SAP BTP cockpit, go to the subaccount where the service binding will be created.

       

    • Access Instances and Subscriptions: Choose Services > Instances and Subscriptions.

 

    • Select Service Instance: Select the service instance created during the initial setup of the cloud edition.

 

    • Create Service Binding: In the Service Bindings section on the right, choose Create and fill in the fields as follows:

 

  • Finalize: Choose Create.

Special Instructions for France (E-Invoices and E-Reports)

If integrating to activate the E-Invoices and E-Reports (France) process, repeat the steps above but select 'Peppol Exchange' as the process instead of 'Other Processes' in the binding configuration.

 

Step 2: Retrieve the OAuth 2.0 Credentials

After creating the service binding, you need to retrieve the credentials to configure the OAuth 2.0 client in your SAP S/4HANA or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition system.

    • Access Instances and Subscriptions — In the SAP BTP cockpit, in the navigation area, choose Services > Instances and Subscriptions.

 

    • Select Service Instance — Under Instances, choose the service instance of the cloud edition. The details screen for the service instance expands on the right side.

 

    • View Credentials — In the details screen, choose a service binding name to open the Credentials screen.

 

  • Retrieve Details — The Credentials screen displays the url, clientId, clientSecret, and drccApiUrl for this service binding.

     

Note: You need these credentials in the next steps when configuring the OAuth 2.0 client.

Summary
You have now successfully created a service binding using OAuth 2.0 client authentication in SAP BTP and retrieved the credentials needed for the integration. The next step is to use the clientId, clientSecret, url, and drccApiUrl to configure the OAuth 2.0 client in your SAP S/4HANA or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition system, which will enable secure communication with SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition.
